files.content:
Ray Hernandez

Designer, builder, founder. 25+ years making things people use.

I started on a Mac in kindergarten (1986) and never stopped building. No college degree, no traditional path — just relentless curiosity and a knack for shipping products people love.

6 companies founded. 1 backed by Mark Cuban. Products used by 40M+ people. Now building AI products that actually work.

Currently: CCO at Vinli, building AI fleet intelligence (Velona AI). Building Recall.Team — AI memory for development teams.

Previously: Inside.com (TLDR newsletters), LikeUs Network (Cuban-backed social), ShopSavvy (40M downloads), Solera (40+ products, 20 countries).

I believe the best products come from people who can see the whole picture — design, engineering, business, and user psychology. That's what I do.

files.name: The Journey.txt
files.content:
The Non-Traditional Path

Age 5: First Mac. Kindergarten, 1986. Something clicked.

High school: Pixel art, early web design, teaching myself everything.

No college. Went straight to work. Got an internship, proved myself, kept going.

First startup at 23. Failed. Learned everything.

Built SocialFly — photo filters before Instagram existed. Built ShopSavvy — 40M people scanning barcodes. Built LikeUs — Mark Cuban wrote the check.

Went corporate at Solera. Learned how to build at scale — 40+ products, 20 countries, 6,500 employees.

Now: AI. Not because it's trendy, but because it's the most important shift since the internet. Building tools that make AI actually useful for real work.

The thread through all of it: I make things. I ship things. I don't wait for permission.

files.name: AI Philosophy.txt
files.content:
My Approach to AI Product Building

Progressive, Not Reactive.

Most companies treat AI as a feature to bolt on. I treat it as a foundation to build from.

Principles:

1. AI should disappear into the workflow.
The best AI products don't feel like "AI products." They feel like better products. If users have to think about the AI, you've failed.

2. Start with the problem, not the model.
Don't ask "what can GPT-4 do?" Ask "what's broken?" Then figure out if AI is the right fix. Sometimes it's not.

3. Ship fast, iterate with real data.
AI products can't be designed in a vacuum. You need real users generating real data to tune the experience. Ship the MVP, watch what happens, adjust.

4. Memory matters more than intelligence.
A slightly dumber AI that remembers context is 10x more useful than a genius that forgets everything between sessions. That's why I built Recall.

5. Human-in-the-loop isn't a compromise.
It's the product. The best AI products amplify human judgment, they don't replace it. Keep humans in control.

What I'm Building Now:
- Recall.Team — Persistent AI memory for development teams
- Velona AI — Fleet intelligence that actually predicts problems
- Custom agent systems — AI that works WITH teams, not instead of them

# Is Traditional SEO Dead?

**Published:** 2024-11-13


**My Thoughts on Navigating the AI Revolution in Search** _(and those thoughts will probably change tomorrow)_

![Image](/assets/stories/358c8610c9.png)

*Yeah it says 2023, but I’m lazy and didn’t want to edit in photoshop. The AI will understand that.*

Ever spent hours agonizing over your website copy, trying to figure out the _exact_ right combination of keywords to magically catapult you to the top of Google’s search results? Yeah, me too. But what if I told you that everything we thought we knew about SEO is about to change?

## The Search Landscape is Shifting (Like, Right Now)

It’s not just the future of SEO we need to worry about; the present is already looking pretty different. Google is spitting out AI-generated answers right at the top of the search results page, ChatGPT has its own search tool, and Perplexity has been doing its AI search thing for a while now.

And it’s not just those big names. A whole swarm of startups are buzzing around, trying to become the next Google _(or maybe even something completely different)_. It’s like the Wild West of search out there, with everyone vying for our attention and our clicks.

![Image](/assets/stories/87f5c62102.jpg)

This means the way people find information is changing _fast_. We’re moving away from the traditional “type keywords, get a list of links” model and towards a more conversational, personalized, and (dare I say) intuitive search experience.

So, what does this mean for us SEO veterans? Well, for starters, it means we can’t just sit back and rely on our old tricks. We need to understand how AI is changing the game and adapt our strategies accordingly. (Don’t worry, I’ll give you some actionable tips later on.)

### The Old Days of Keyword Stuffing (RIP)

Remember when we used to cram our websites with keywords like it was nobody’s business? “Best widgets,” “buy widgets now,” “widgets for sale” — plastered _everywhere_. _(Okay, maybe it wasn’t our proudest moment)._

But the thing is, **AI doesn’t just read words; it _understands_ them.** It’s like Google suddenly got a whole lot smarter. But not like smarter, like can read your damn mind, smarter.

## AI’s Superpowers: SEO Just Got a Whole Lot Smarter _(And Maybe a Little Terrifying)_

Okay, let’s face it. AI is kind of freaking everyone out these days. But before you start picturing robots taking over the world (or at least stealing our SEO jobs), let’s take a deep breath and break down what AI can _actually_ do when it comes to search.

## Seeing is Believing (Literally)

Remember those days of meticulously crafting alt text for every image on your website? You know, trying to cram in all the relevant keywords while still sounding somewhat human? Yeah, AI is making all that effort look a bit silly.

**Why?** Because AI can actually _see_ what’s in those images. It’s not just recognizing basic shapes and colors anymore; it’s understanding the context, the emotions, the whole shebang. It’s like Google Images suddenly got a PhD in art history.

![Image](/assets/stories/eca5d31c0c.jpg)

Think about it: if you have a picture of a dog playing fetch on a beach, AI can not only identify the dog, the beach, and the action, but it can also recognize the joyful expression on the dog’s face. It’s like Google has become a dog whisperer (but, you know, for images).

This means Google can understand the context of your images even if your alt text game is weak. And that’s just the beginning.

## From Keywords to Context (Mind Reading 101)

Keywords are _so_ 2023. AI is all about understanding the context, tone, and intent behind your words. It’s like Google suddenly learned how to read between the lines (and maybe even detect a hint of sarcasm).

For example, if you search for “best running shoes,” Google’s AI can now figure out if you’re a casual jogger looking for comfortable sneakers or a marathon runner who needs high-performance footwear. It’s like Google can peek into your soul _(but in a non-invasive, GDPR-compliant way, of course)_.

This means you can’t just stuff your content with keywords anymore and hope for the best. You need to write for humans _and_ for AI, which means focusing on clear, concise, and engaging language that accurately reflects your intent.

## Search That Actually Gets You (AI with Empathy?)

Remember those times when you’d search for something specific, only to be bombarded with irrelevant results? Frustrating, right? Well, AI is stepping up to change that — and it’s not just about matching keywords anymore.

**AI is learning to understand us on a whole new level.** It’s not just reading what we type; it’s picking up on context, intent, and even our emotions. Imagine you’re searching for “best ways to relax after a long day.” Instead of just listing generic relaxation techniques, the AI senses that you’re stressed and might suggest a calming playlist, a nearby yoga class, or even recommend a funny video to lift your spirits.

Or let’s say you’re shopping for a new pair of sneakers. The AI knows your favorite colors from your past searches and browsing history. So, it shows you options in shades you love, potentially improving your mood as you shop. It’s like having a personal assistant who not only knows what you’re looking for but also understands how you’re feeling.

**Sounds crazy? It’s closer than you think.**

![Image](/assets/stories/e14ca51719.jpg)

AI algorithms are becoming so sophisticated that they can analyze images, recognize objects, and even detect emotions. They understand the difference between a smiling face and a frown, a serene landscape and a chaotic scene. This means search engines can tailor results not just based on what you ask for, but how you might be feeling at the moment.

Think about it: if two websites offer the same product, AI might direct you to the one with a design that resonates more with your personal aesthetic or even the one that uses colors known to boost mood. It’s search that doesn’t just get you — it _feels_ you.

**This changes everything for SEO.**

It’s no longer just about having the right keywords or backlinks. It’s about creating content and experiences that connect on a human level. Google used to just index the internet; now, AI is beginning to understand what that index actually _means_.

_Why does this matter for us?_ Because as AI continues to evolve, the way people find and interact with content will shift dramatically. To stay ahead, we need to focus on creating authentic, emotionally resonant content that truly serves our audience’s needs — not just their search queries.

> **The Future of SEO: What’s Next? (Hold on Tight!)**

The SEO landscape is about to get a whole lot more interesting. Here’s a glimpse into the future:

## Feeling Your Pain (Empathetic Search Engines)

Imagine a search engine that can sense your mood. If you search for “how to deal with a breakup,” it might prioritize a blog post with an empathetic and supportive tone over a dry, clinical article. Get ready for search results that _feel_ your pain.

This means we need to start thinking about the emotional impact of our content. Are we connecting with our audience on a deeper level? Are we providing support, inspiration, or humor when it’s needed?

## Judging Your Content (The AI Professor)

![Image](/assets/stories/5e23cf8bd3.jpg)

AI will soon be judging your content like a grumpy English professor. It’ll assess readability, structure, originality, and even the credibility of your sources. Say goodbye to those days of churning out thin, keyword-stuffed content.

This means we need to step up our content game. We need to invest in high-quality, well-researched content that truly delivers value. Tools like Grammarly, SurferSEO, Quill are already using AI to analyze content for readability, grammar, and plagiarism.

## Talking to Our Devices (Conversational Search)

With the rise of voice assistants like Siri and Alexa, we’re talking to our devices more than ever. This means optimizing for conversational queries is no longer optional — it’s essential. People don’t talk like robots, so your content shouldn’t either.


This means we need to start writing in a more natural, conversational style. Think about how people actually talk and ask questions. Start by incorporating long-tail keywords and question-and-answer formats into your content.

## The SEO Survival Guide (Don’t Panic!)

Okay, deep breaths. The AI revolution might seem daunting, but it’s not the end of the world. Here’s how to ride the wave:

## 1\. Quality Over Quantity (Always)

Remember those keyword-stuffed articles you used to churn out? Yeah, AI can smell those a mile away. Focus on creating in-depth, comprehensive content that provides real value. Think deep dives, ultimate guides, and well-researched articles.

_Actionable Step:_ Start by auditing your existing content to identify and improve thin or outdated articles.

## 2\. Multimedia is Your Friend

Spice things up with images, videos, infographics, and even podcasts. AI loves a good multimedia experience (and so do your users). Just make sure it’s all high-quality and relevant — AI can spot a filler image from a mile away.

_Actionable Step:_ Experiment with different multimedia formats to see what resonates with your audience.

## 3\. Understand What Your Audience Really Wants

Forget about cramming keywords. Focus on understanding what your audience is _really_ searching for and tailor your content to meet their needs. Think less about keywords and more about solutions.

_Actionable Step:_ Use tools like \[Tool Name\] to analyze search intent and identify relevant topics.

## 4\. User Experience is Key

A fast-loading, mobile-friendly website with intuitive navigation isn’t just user-friendly; it’s AI-friendly. Search engines will factor in UX more heavily in rankings, so make sure your website is a joy to use.

_Actionable Step:_ Conduct a website audit to identify and fix any UX issues.

## 5\. Keep it Real (and Ethical)

Don’t try to game the system with shady tactics. AI is smarter than that, and it’ll catch you red-handed. Focus on building genuine authority and trust with your audience.

_Actionable Step:_ Familiarize yourself with Google’s latest guidelines and best practices for SEO.

## Confessions of an SEO Veteran (I’ve Seen Some Things…)

Let’s be real, I’ve been in the SEO/SEM game for a while now. I’ve seen algorithms change like the wind, and strategies that went from hot to not in the blink of an eye. I mean, I even used to create fake MySpace profiles back in the day, complete with strategically placed links to affiliate products. _(Hey, a teenager’s gotta eat, right?)_

Those were the Good Ole Days of SEO & Marketing, where you could get away with almost anything. But AI is changing all that. It’s like the sheriff finally rolled into town.

And now? It feels like everything I’ve learned is about to get flipped on its head.

> **But hey, at least it’s keeping me on my toes, right?**

## The Good News

*   **Innovation Keeps Us Sharp**: This AI wave is forcing us to evolve, think creatively, and focus on what truly matters: providing value to our audience.
*   **Better User Experience**: Ultimately, these changes are making the internet a better place for everyone. And that’s something we can all get behind.

## The Challenging Part

*   **The Learning Curve**: Adapting to new technologies can be tough. It takes time, effort, and a willingness to unlearn old habits (like keyword stuffing). I’ve personally had to spend hours learning (really playing) with every AI tool that comes out. I watch AI news in the morning, play with AI tools in the afternoon, and by the evening most nights, I’ve created something new. A blog post, an app, learned about a new thing I didn’t know about, or brainstormed ideas I have in my head. This is how I stay ahead of the curve.
*   **Uncertainty**: With AI evolving at warp speed, it’s hard to predict what’s next. Strategies might become obsolete faster than a viral meme. But that’s also what makes this so exciting — it’s a constant challenge to stay ahead of the game.

## Let’s Navigate This Together (We’re All in This)

So, is traditional SEO dead? Maybe not entirely, but it’s definitely getting a serious makeover. And as daunting as that sounds, it’s also a massive opportunity.

**Ask yourself:**

> **Are you ready to ditch the gimmicks and focus on genuine value?**
> 
> **Will you embrace the AI revolution and ride the wave of change?**
> 
> **Can you adapt your strategies to meet the needs of a smarter search engine (and a smarter audience)?**

If you answered “**yes**,” then buckle up. It’s going to be a wild ride.

## Key Takeaways (TL;DR)

*   **Keywords Aren’t King Anymore**: AI cares about context and content depth, not just keyword density.
*   **Quality Content Wins**: Provide real value, and AI will reward you (and so will your audience).
*   **User Experience Matters**: From site speed to mobile optimization, UX is crucial for both users and AI.
*   **Stay Adaptable**: The only constant is change. Be ready to pivot and evolve your strategies.

## Final Thoughts (Let’s Do This!)

The AI revolution in SEO isn’t something to fear; it’s something to embrace. Sure, it’s shaking things up, but it’s also leveling the playing field. Those who focus on quality, authenticity, and user experience will thrive in this new era.

So let’s roll up our sleeves, ditch the keyword stuffing, and get to work creating amazing content that truly connects with our audience. The future of SEO is here, and it’s powered by AI. Let’s make it awesome.

_What are your thoughts on the AI shake-up in SEO/SEM, hell just in life? Let’s chat! Drop a comment below or hit me up on Twitter/X @raydawg88_

# Kondo for Mac: Transforming Desktops from Cluttered to Productive

**Published:** 2024-06-04


_Automate and tame your crazy desktop with 1 simple click_

**“FML” —** that was my daily mantra each time I opened my MacBook. I’m Ray, a UX Director with a paradox: a spotless physical desk and a digital workspace that was a total mess.

![Image](/assets/stories/e389ad072b.png)

*Yes, this “was” my actual desktop — Be sure not to miss the stacking in the top right corner*

**The ADHD Challenge:** In the world of design and technology, where creativity meets constant innovation, ADHD can be a double-edged sword. It fuels extraordinary creativity but also creates a unique set of challenges. Distractions multiply, and the digital workspace can quickly become overwhelming — a cluttered, chaotic environment where focus goes to die. For

[Steven Ray](/u/11b50604d273?source=post_page---user_mention--4d063c282286---------------------------------------)

and myself, the founders of Kondo, grappling with ADHD meant our digital spaces were battlegrounds of unfinished ideas and unorganized files, directly impacting our productivity and mental clarity.

That’s when we decided to take control and create Kondo. Think Marie Kondo, but for your digital life. Kondo is not just another organization tool; it’s a way to rejuvenate your digital environment.

> **_“_Your environment, including your digital one, deeply influences your mental and emotional well-being._” — Nick Huberman_**

When we started decluttering our digital files, the change was palpable. Clearing out the digital clutter didn’t just tidy up our screens; it uncluttered our minds.


Kondo is our solution to a problem many of us face but often overlook. It’s perfect for anyone from creatives losing track of their files, to students swamped with digital notes, to professionals seeking some digital zen.

Influential thinkers have long talked about the link between our environment and our mental state. A cluttered space leads to a cluttered mind. Kondo is our way of applying this wisdom to our digital lives, helping to bring order and tranquility to the chaos of files and folders.

If your digital world feels more like a jungle than a haven, it’s time to try Kondo. We’re on a mission to transform digital spaces, making them more organized and, consequently, making you more calm and productive.

![Image](/assets/stories/7383a7aa2f.png)

We’re excited to see how Kondo evolves, and we’re always eager to hear your stories of digital clutter and triumph. # My 7 year old daughter wrote her first song

**Published:** 2020-01-30


## And it’s pretty good (not just saying that cause i’m her dad)


![Image](/assets/stories/6f1612eed7.jpg)

*Era Moon Hernandez*

> IF you’re impatient…and you just want to jump straight to the final edited song here it is…
> 
> **THUNDER and RAIN  
> **[https://soundcloud.com/user-123885966/thunder-and-rain](https://soundcloud.com/user-123885966/thunder-and-rain)

A couple of years ago my family decided we weren’t going to do the traditional Christmas anymore. You know, the tons of gifts under the tree, and kids just telling us all the random things they **have to have** this year. It just didn’t feel special. 1 it costs a ton of money, and 2 the kids really don’t get anything of value out of it.

### Obviously we still did presents…we aren’t monsters

Even though we didn’t want presents to be the focus, I still wanted to do something special for my kids. A gift that you couldn’t just buy at the mall. So I started thinking about what that could be for my daughter. Her latest interest, which I think came from us watching the TV show [Songland](https://www.nbc.com/songland) as a family together, has been songwriting. She’s always been into music, we even used lyrics as a way to teach our kids how to read. We’d print out the lyrics, and have them practice reading a song they were familiar with or liked.


So anyway, she’s been writing a lot of songs lately, and I thought what if I had an artist take one of her songs, and actual produce it into a legit sounding radio-ready song.

> “what if I had an artist take one of her songs, and actual produce it into a legit sounding radio-ready song”

So I searched through a ton of musicians, songwriters, singers, etc on [Fiverr](https://www.fiverr.com/), and finally came across one that I really liked. Her name was [Emma](https://www.fiverr.com/emmamaidenberg/write-and-record-lyrics-for-you?context=recommendation&context_alg=recently_ordered%7Cco&context_referrer=homepage&context_type=gig&mod=hap&pckg_id=3&pos=1&tier_selection=recommended&ref_ctx_id=9a446abf-4b4a-4a25-b1c5-bc702ebb6025) and her examples were really good, and something that I could totally see my daughter listening to. I sent her a message, and told her what I was trying to do with one of my daughters songs, and she was so into it. She even had all of these ideas about how they could do skype chats and she could teach her about songwriting — which I want to do in the future, but for this to be a surprise I thought we’d do this like ninjas this time.

It was such a fun process. It first started with just sending her Era’s lyrics, so she could start getting some ideas of how to compose the music for the song.

![Image](/assets/stories/931198c5a9.jpg)

*First draft of Thunder and Rain lyrics*

Emma would have questions about specific lines in the song that Era wrote, and I’d slyly ask my daughter what she thinking when she said that, and then I’d video her answers. I’d send the recordings to Emma, and then she’d make edits or additions to the song to make it complete.

![Image](/assets/stories/8f0abb9428.png)

Emma would make some changes to the arrangement, and then a few days later she sent me a message with the lyrics broken down into a song structure:

> **Thunder and Rain**
> 
> What do you hear?  
> I hear lovely tears  
> I go outside, What do I see?  
> Thunder and rain  
> Thunder and rain
> 
> **Verse 1**  
> Open the window, I see the light  
> but I don’t want to go outside  
> I want thunder and rain  
> thunder and rain
> 
> it’s Winter in california and  
> im staring out at the lake  
> The air is clear outside and  
> I wish I could take  
> This feeling home with me  
> Thunder and rain
> 
> **Chorus**  
> What do you hear?  
> I hear lovely tears  
> I go outside, What do I see?  
> Thunder and rain  
> Thunder and rain
> 
> **Verse 2**  
> I want to say lets go play in the rain  
> In the mud, the trees, and in the lake  
> Im inside with my family  
> The fireplace crackling
> 
> Rain on the window  
> Dancing up a storm  
> But inside I feel so warm  
> I love thunder and rain  
> Thunder and rain
> 
> **Chorus**  
> What do you hear?  
> I hear lovely tears  
> I go outside, What do I see?  
> Thunder and rain  
> Thunder and rain

The next day she sent me a recording with the piano and her voice over the top, and it blew my mind. I mean what she did was amazing, but the speed she put all of this together was at another level.

Like I said before, this was an amazing experience, and I’m such a proud father. I love that my daughter is creative, and hopefully with presents like this, she knows that she can work in collaboration with people to make great things. I can’t wait to see what she comes up with in the future.

I love you Era! And just want you to know you have a really proud dad who thinks you can do anything!

### ANYWAY…enough mushy mush stuff…lets get to it!

Era’s first single…**THUNDER and RAIN  
**[https://soundcloud.com/user-123885966/thunder-and-rain](https://soundcloud.com/user-123885966/thunder-and-rain)

**Follow her on Soundcloud here  
**[https://soundcloud.com/user-123885966](https://soundcloud.com/user-123885966)

## AND if you don’t mind…leave her a comment on there. I’m gonna start sharing stats, and comments with her so that she can see that people all over the world are actually listening to something she wrote.

_Just want to say thanks again to Emma who was amazing in this process, and really think that you’ve helped inspire my daughter to keep writing and pursuing music._

> My son’s Christmas present will be another post that I’ll post here shortly. It’s pretty cool too.

files.content:
# Gimme a BREAK (app)

**Published:** 2018-10-26


## Take a break without being judged by your coworkers


![Image](/assets/stories/6ebfeca3ba.gif)

*“FML”*

I started a new job as a Director of UX a few months back for a pretty large global corporation. I inherited a few designers, as well as a giant backlog of tasks. Needless to say my team was over capacity and crazy busy with tasks from day 1.

A few weeks into working there one of the designers on my UX team made a comment that I just couldn’t get out of my head. She said “I really wish I had time to read all of the articles you all post in slack, but I honestly am so busy that I never have 5 minutes to just sit there and take a break”. As her boss, this really upset me. **1** cause she’s being overworked and that’s just not how people can perform quality work and **2** everyone should have 5 minutes in their day to do whatever they want to do…read an article, watch a video, etc. (actually it’s kinda the law)

> **She explained,** “it’s not that I’m not allowed to take a break, I just work with a lot of _old school_ employees that basically make comments about how I’m slacking off at work if they see me watching a video or reading an article.”

## Hearing her say this, got my blood boiling.

No one should be killing themselves at work, and then when they take a breather be made to feel like they are not doing their job.


Since my brain wouldn’t let this go, I started thinking through this from a product perspective. Basically I was determined to solve this problem for her. I realized that there’s not a lot of times where employees don’t see watching youtube videos at work as slacking off. Then I had a bit of a lightbulb.

## There is one time when no one questions why you’re not working…and actually they sympathize that you’re having to deal with it.

![Image](/assets/stories/22e7ace40a.jpg)

**When your computer is updating. You’re completely locked out, and there’s nothing you can do about it. Only thing you can do is wait.** This is the one time at work where not only are you not seen as a slacker at work, but you’re completely open to do anything you want to do for a specified amount of time. Watch that youtube video, read that medium post, see what’s trending on twitter, or just get up and go for a walk. And yes…I do realize how strange it is for a manager to be figuring out a way for their employee to take a break and not work, but I’ve seen first hand how improved a person can be just by taking a mental break here and there.

![Image](/assets/stories/55822d915d.jpg)

### This is your **solid black get out of jail free card.**

## WHAT IS THE POINT OF THIS STORY?!?!

**I made an** [**app**](https://itunes.apple.com/us/app/break-app/id1438515820?mt=12) to fix this problem for my designer, but you can use it too!

![Image](/assets/stories/187a07195d.png)

I launched the [**BREAK**](https://itunes.apple.com/us/app/break-app/id1438515820?mt=12) app today on [Product Hunt](https://www.producthunt.com/posts/break-f9a08ed8-10fe-4772-a0fa-97f267e48f93), and it’s now available on the [Mac App Store](https://itunes.apple.com/us/app/break-app/id1438515820?mt=12). It’s super simple, and I hope it’s super helpful to anyone who works with those “judgy folks”.

**You just:**  
• Open the App  
• Click the ghost in your menu bar  
• Select your break time _(5, 10, 15, 30 minutes)_  
• Click the start button  
•TAKE YOUR BREAK

## [DOWNLOAD THE BREAK APP FOR MAC NOW!](https://itunes.apple.com/us/app/break-app/id1438515820?mt=12)

## Your brain will thank you.

> LIL UPDATE: Apple rejected the app 4 times, since I was using their apple icon to make the loading screen look super legit…SOOOOOO…I just made it where you can add whatever icon you want for the loading screen (gifs as well)…and now it’s a lot more fun. So make it legit if you want, or get crazy with it.

> I’d love to hear your thoughts, and would love to hear from anyone who gets some value out of the BREAK app. I have some ideas for a V2, but obviously I want to build what you would want. So let me know what you think I should add.

files.content:
# The Unicorn Company

**Published:** 2018-04-20


$6 billion dollar valuation with a 6 year old Founder

![Image](/assets/stories/70a61ca04e.png)

*Era Moon Hernandez — The Unicorn Lady*

My daughter, Era Moon Hernandez, is a super creative little 6 year old. She loves to draw, and is always coming up with amazing ideas. The thing is…and yes…I know it’s super weird to be thinking about this already…BUT…**I don’t really want my daughter to go to college** (unless she really wants to). I’ve always wanted her to already have a profitable company before she graduates high school, so she would be faced with the dilemma of — _“Do I go to college or do I grow this company?”_

This has been on my mind a lot lately, so this week I asked her…

> “Era, if you were going to start your own business what would it be?”

She didn’t even take 2 seconds to think about it before she just blurted out **“UNICORNS!!!”** I asked her what she wanted to do with Unicorns, and she told me that she “really really really loves drawing unicorns.” I then asked her…

> “Do you know you could start a unicorn company right now?”

She looked at me with that super cute puzzled look, and said

> “Daddy, I’m not a grown up, I can’t have a company”.

She said it so cute, but it actually made me sad. I knew I had to resolve this…so I told her we can start her own company right now. All we need is a product, and since you love drawing unicorns lets turn those drawings into a product. She asked me to help her, and that’s when [The Unicorn Company](https://www.unicorncompany.co) was born.

**We got to work.** She started drawing unicorns, and I started working on her website. I used squarespace to get something up quick, and after she was done with a few drawings we would take product photos together. Then after we uploaded it to the site I would ask her to describe her drawing for me so I could use her own words as the product description. When you go to [her site](https://www.unicorncompany.co) and read the product descriptions you’ll see what I mean. I kept in all her…umms…and mmmms…and ohs…in there so you can really hear it from her mouth.


If you can’t wait here’s my favorite example for this product called — [Unicorn Human](https://www.unicorncompany.co/collection/a-unicorn-human)

> Ummm…it has 3 horns. Because I think it’ll look cool like that. Umm…her dress is purple…i think it looks pretty…like that. Umm…she has 3 legs…because things can be like different…like she has 3 horns…she has 3 legs. THINGS CAN BE DIFFERENT.

So tonight I got her site finished, and am about ready to launch it, but I started thinking about all the things I want her to learn about this process. Here’s a quick rundown of what I hope she takes away from this project:

*   **Things she creates have real value**
*   **There is a process — create the art, sell the art, ship the art** (and I will have her do all of these steps herself, down to licking the stamp)
*   **There is a big world out there. My hope is that I can get someone to buy one of her drawings from somewhere else in the world, so she can see how the internet connects people globally**
*   **Selfishly, I want her to know what her daddy does** (design, build products, etc)
*   **I want her to also understand how hard having your own business is. It’s not always fun, but if you love what you do it’s worth it**
*   **I want her to understand how hard her mama worked to build her own spa**
*   **Most importantly I want her to have fun, be creative, and see how fun being an entrepreneur is**

I’ll end with a quick message to my daughter, that hopefully she can fully read all by yourself here in a few months. (she can read a lil bit right now, but she’s still learning)

> Era,
> 
> Daddy loves you so much, and even if you don’t sell a single unicorn drawing, I seriously loved working on this project with you. It makes me so happy to watch you draw, watch you be creative, and to be super excited to build your company. I’m super proud, and excited for what you’re building & whatever you’re gonna build in the future.

![Image](/assets/stories/046c7a1e90.png)

## [Check Out — The Unicorn Company](https://www.unicorncompany.co)

**Update:** She’s on Product Hunt Today — [https://medium.com/@raydawg88/the-unicorn-company-d620d56c075c](/@raydawg88/the-unicorn-company-d620d56c075c)

files.content:
# HURRY THE F*CK UP

**Published:** 2015-10-19

# HURRY THE F\*CK UP

## It doesn’t take that long to build quality products


![Image](/assets/stories/670a701147.png)

> (This is a talk I gave at Launch Scale 2015 at Fort Mason in San Franscisco)

Hey hey…My name is [Ray Hernandez](https://www.stoodio.com) and I work for that guy over there you may have heard of, [Jason Calacanis](https://twitter.com/jason). I’m the Head of Product over at [Inside.com](https://www.inside.com)…and I wanted to come and chat with everyone…and share a lil process that our team uses to build products in a matter of days vs weeks/months.

But before we get into that…I wanted to explain this slide. This is my son…Hawk. That’s a pretty badass name huh? So I started with him 1 cause he loves seeing his face on the TV…but 2 cause he teaches me a lot about product everyday.

He’s young…he probably has a.d.d. since I do…and if you give him a toy, an app, anything and it doesn’t work the way he expected it. He smashes it into million pieces. And i’m serious…look at my phone…it’s in pieces. When you step back…that’s pretty much what we do for a living. We make apps…sites…things… that we don‘t want people to smash.

![Image](/assets/stories/4cdf26e294.png)

That’s a big part of what I wanted to share with everyone. The typical process when you build a product…is you have this amazing idea. You take that idea…and brainstorm more amazing ideas. You spend a bunch of time designing concepts…you refine those…then you build them. THEN…you finally show it to someone and ask them if they like it.

Our team tweaks this order, and avoid the smashing from potential customers.

![Image](/assets/stories/7bb486ce8d.png)

So when I was getting ready for this talk…I wanted to see how long the rest of the world thought just building an iOS app should take. I found this awesome infographic that says based on 100 surveyed developers that the avg time to build an app is 18 weeks. 18 fucking weeks! Holy crap! Now I’ve worked at agencies that we’ve spent way longer than that, and I’ve been a freelancer where you drag things out just so you can get a big pay check…but damn. I mean look at this…it says that also is the same amount of time it takes to build 1.3 Boeing 777 Planes….this one is my favorite cause it’s a riduculous stat. Build 1 / 231th of the Giza Pyramid. Someone sat there and calculated that.

BUT…I bring this up for one core reason.

## **It’s all bullshit.**

![Image](/assets/stories/435ac0c107.png)

## It’s bullshit cause if you plan…do things in the right order…you can knock out high quality stuff so freakin fast.

![Image](/assets/stories/7779a6fe14.png)

When I tell people we build these products so fast they all tend to go “how the fuck did you do that?” So here’s the answer. And it’s a lot simpler than you think.

![Image](/assets/stories/b1dc16e38a.png)

*Yes…this slide is suppose to look ugly.*

It’s called **THE UGLY FLOW**. Our team gets together and brainstorms an idea. We make a list of the goals, the actions, and anything we want to ad. From there we grab a fancy piece of paper. We’ll sketch up UI…and nothing fancy…we’re just making sure we have a spot for everything. From there we move over to photoshop/sketch and make a digital version of the sketch. Now one thing to note…we’re not making this beautiful. This is hard as a designer…you want to make it sexy as hell…but chill the fuck back. Make it ugly on purpose. Use comic sans, pick photos that are just random, and copy and paste you’re favorite ipsum text. I’m a big fan of Cupcake Ipsum.


Once you have an ugly digital version…you throw it into [InVision](https://www.invisionapp.com). We all know [InVision](https://www.invisionapp.com) right? They let you make badass prototypes! But…the real value we get from [InVision](https://www.invisionapp.com) is that we can instantly get people talking about the product. And when I say people…I don’t mean my team. They don’t get to play right now, it’s not their turn. Right now you want people outside of your company. The meaner the better. If your grandma has ever said “you look really fat today” she’s gonna be a great tester. If your neighbor every told you “that dress just isn’t cute” she’s gonna be a great tester. Get people that will give you real feedback…not just pat you on the back and tell you how great you are.

## Why do we do this? Cause we don’t have all the answers. Let me say that again **“We don’t have all the answers!”**

![Image](/assets/stories/14ad734be7.png)

*Get into InVision as quickly as possible, and ask people what they want.*

**Now let me give you a warning here.** You’re gonna get some of the dumbest responses you’ve ever heard. People will focus on that random kitten photo you picked, and how you spelled Ipsum wrong…BUT…once you weed through all of that you’re gonna get these little golden nuggets. Your aunt is gonna say, it’s really hard to read, I wish the font was bigger and not so grey. And you think damn…our core demographic is 25–45 year olds…and my aunt is 36. So if she’s having trouble with it…then some other 40+ users will too.

![Image](/assets/stories/2b4347fa1f.png)

So now you have all of these lil golden nuggets coming in…and it’s your team’s turn to play. Our team starts going through the comments and dissecting them. And that’s when the magic starts happening. We’re out of your own box, so we start realizing all the things we missed…what we didn’t think about adding…what people want from a product like this….and now we know what we need to.

![Image](/assets/stories/bbfc21d61d.png)

*Designers and Developers on the same page…yeah it happens (sometimes).*

One really big thing I want to point out here. EVERYONE IS ON THE SAME PAGE. Devs and designers are never on the same page…especially when it comes in the design phase. You’re always gonna have a difference of opinion…it’s like epic street fighter 2 battle. But…having these outside people…who are potential customers…comment about the product AND LITERALLY JUST TELL YOU WHAT TO BUILD…kinda squashes the beef and just makes everyone feel like we’re building for them…not for us.

![Image](/assets/stories/649f81d2ee.png)

So lets look at where we are. We have spent a whopping total of 4–5 hours and we’re already refining our design, planning our build, and rolling on this new product. It really is that simple. If you ask your customers “what would you like us to build you” vs “do you like this thing I spent all this time building”…you will save time…money…and have to digest a lot less tylenol.

![Image](/assets/stories/15a340f33a.png)

So I know I said the word “bullshit” a lot…and this technique probably sounds that way to you. But seriously…it’s not. If you just ask your potential customers what they want, and then build that. You’ll always save time, money, and have less grey hair.

Using this flow…Our team has designed and developed a native iOS app, Chrome Extension, and new Website in 3 weeks. And that’s all tied into a giant curation system, with a massive API.

![Image](/assets/stories/ffcac3a5a9.png)

I’ll leave you with this awesome quote…that I made up:

> It’s easy to build a product…it’s hard as hell to build a “good product”. It’s even harder to build a good product quickly. It’s crazy fucking hard…to build a good product fast, unless you ask your customers what they want upfront.

**Thank to @jason for letting me participate at Launch Scale 2015.**

# TLDR 2.0 — BITE SIZE NEWS IN EVERY TAB

**Published:** 2015-12-04

![Image](/assets/stories/e2f4ddfd0c.png)


## GOING FROM AN EXPERIMENT TO A PRODUCT


Only 30 days ago, we launched the first version of the [TLDR Chrome Extension](https://bitly.com/1IFiABL) on [Product Hunt](https://www.producthunt.com/). We actually held that top spot for the majority of the day, but eventually lost out to Twitter because well… Twitter.

![Image](/assets/stories/6c94d1bc49.png)

*Our first launch of TLDR on Product Hunt*

Starting that day, and then every day since, we’ve heard from users who love the product. They tell us that we broke important stories to them they hadn’t heard anywhere else. That we’re keeping them up-to-date on topics they didn’t really know too much about before. A few of them have even said it makes them look forward to the surprises that await them every time they open a new tab.

Hearing things like that makes you feel great when you build products for a living.

## TAKING IT A STEP FURTHER

Right around Launch Day, we added a service called Intercom to our extension, allowing us to directly ask users for feedback, feature requests, things they hated or loved or wanted, and so forth.

It only took us a few days to talk with several hundred people, and that’s when we started to see a pattern emerge. Our users wanted more control over what kinds of news we sent them.

TLDR had originally been envisioned as essentially a firehose of news, pointed at your browser. We just turn it on and suddenly, you’re overwhelmed with every kind of popular, trending, important story our curators can find, from every category and vertical possible.

But what we heard from users was, that firehose needed a spigot. Some people wanted us to ditch the fluff and concentrate on hard news. Others don’t care about U.S. news and wanted stories from their part of the world. Many people wrote us saying that they wanted a version of TLDR but only about their favorite subject. What would it look like with just Tech? Only World News? Sports and Business but not Politics?

Almost everyone we heard from agreed that TLDR, as a concept, had value. But few of them agreed, specifically, on what the content should be like. So we made two decisions:

1.  This silly TLDR experiment was worthwhile and we should go all-in to really give our users the complete experience they wanted.
2.  The most effective way to do that for the most people would be to give them more control, and the ability to customize their feeds.

Of course, it’s all still a work in progress, but here are the features we’ve added to this first big update.


**FILTERING**

We’ve divided all of our TLDR content into two very general buckets.

“NEWS” refers to real-time stories of significance. Think major world events like wars, diplomatic incidents, elections and natural disasters. This is where we put all the important stuff you really ought to read.

“FLUFF” refers to the fun stuff. Trending and “viral” Internet content, TV and movie updates, music videos, news about celebrities and, of course, Cats.

A LOT of people told us they wanted more News and less Fluff. BUT we also see what kinds of links people click on, when they don’t know we’re watching. So we decided to give everyone a choice. You can leave the controls alone and see Everything, or you can turn on Just News. Or, you can turn on Just Fluff, if you’re having a bad day and just need to enjoy adorable things and Crossfit fails.

**LIST VIEW**

A lot of users found the GIFs and images overwhelming, and wanted a more clean, Reddit-style view. So we’ve added that as an option.

**CURATED RSS READER**

A lot of people told us they wanted more of a specific kind of news. Sports came up a number of times. So did Finance. But we didn’t get enough of a consensus to really direct our editorial team. Many people want to know about many different things.

So what we’ve done instead is add a simple RSS reader to TLDR. Our editorial team picked a few of our favorite blogs and websites from a number of highly requested categories, and you can now add them as a compliment to our main TLDR feed.

**SHARING**

When designing TLDR, we had wanted to give it a very clean, streamlined design, making the links the focus, rather than the product itself. One early decision we made was to drop “share” buttons. We were a bit surprised to have so many people ask us to put them back! But “I need a way to share this with my friends” seemed to come up a bunch, so we’ve added a button to help you easily share the original article to Twitter. (We figured we’d start with just one and see how it goes, and maybe add more social media platforms down the road.)

Along with these new features, we also cleaned up the design (and our code) quite a bit. We think the page is much cleaner, and we know that stories load 80% faster than the previous version. (Another common piece of feedback we heard from users: LOAD FASTER!)

![Image](/assets/stories/5ccd7ec3a1.png)

*The new and improved TLDR 2.0*

### We are super pumped about TLDR 2.0 and hope you all love it as much as we do!

[**Install the new TLDR Google Chrome Extension Now**](https://bitly.com/1IFiABL)

# TLDR — The News You Want Right F-ing Now

**Published:** 2015-10-28

![Image](/assets/stories/2146cb1a1b.png)


If you follow anyone from [Inside.com](https://www.inside.com) on social media, or just stood within a few hundred yards of us this week, you probably heard about our NEW APP! It’s called **TLDR** and it’s great and you should [download it](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/tldr-new-tab-news-right-f/bgnfebilkfnllimjokbkniclpjdajlag) because it shows you everything that’s going on in the news in one place, remarkably fast, and in a fun, irreverent way that won’t bore you to death.

So anyway, download that. It’s available for [iOS](https://itunes.apple.com/us/app/inside-tl-dr-24-7-breaking/id1042749369?mt=8) and [Google Chrome](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/tldr-new-tab-news-right-f/bgnfebilkfnllimjokbkniclpjdajlag).  
**We like it. You’ll like it. Is good.**

### But that’s not why I’m writing this blog post. I’m writing this because we worked pretty hard to spread the word about TLDR online this week, and had some interesting results which we wanted to share.

Probably the biggest single impact on our launch was posting our app to Product Hunt and winding up in the #1 spot there for the morning and some of the afternoon. This was no accident, but was something we deliberately planned our launch around. Our CEO, [Jason Calacanis](https://twitter.com/jason), even stayed up late the night before, to post the app to the site around midnight, then hang out with their community and answer questions.

![Image](/assets/stories/6c7e8291f7.png)

*#1 on Product Hunt*

Which turned out to be really important! Getting ranked on Product Hunt is good for some downloads. And it just feels great to refresh the page and see upvotes coming in for what you’ve been working on so long. But interacting with the community directly, getting people there to not just check out TLDR but get EXCITED about it, was the big win. [Jason](https://twitter.com/jason) turned the comment thread into an impromptu AMA, and people hung around for a long time to engage with him, even downloading the app sometimes so they could ask better questions.

That creates users who will not just flip through TLDR once, then get back to Mountain Goat Mountain. They’ll come back, because now it has a personal meaning and connection for them. And we’re hoping to continue that tradition within the app itself, using easy feedback and communication tools to keep in constant touch with our community of users.

(For a news curation app, a feedback loop like this is particularly crucial. How else am I going to know what kind of news people want to see covered? I mean, obviously, they want to hear about when it’s International Caps Lock Day ([https://www.esquire.com/news-politics/news/a39068/caps-lock-day/](https://www.esquire.com/news-politics/news/a39068/caps-lock-day/)). But, you know, for other stuff.

![Image](/assets/stories/f6643f1553.png)

The best overall news from the day came from the iOS Store. Our app’s page got 1,170 views and 556 installs, a 48% install rate. This showed significant improvement from when we originally launched our first product, Inside.com and started getting press. We’ve totally rethought our approach to our download page in the iTunes Store, abandoning dry descriptions of the product in plain English and opting for a tongue-in-cheek, even silly approach.

> (We had some pushback to writing the description with humor, and including web-friendly slang and phrasing like “right f-ing now” or the expression TL;DR itself, but putting things in a more casual, conversational tone definitely seemed to help goose the download numbers a bit. It just makes the app seem like it’s going to be more fun to use. Eat24, which has such an approachable, engaging tone of voice in public communications, was the main inspiration.)

We also used Mailchimp to send out an email blast to Inside fans about the new release. Reaching out to all of these people at once, and letting them know that we had a new product in a fast, fun way, did make a difference, but not as big as we had anticipated.


Part of the reason may have been the new, snarkier tone of voice, which played great in the app store but maybe not via email. Only 7% of the people we emailed with the “Right F-ing Now” subject line actually opened the email, about half the industry average. Oops. (On the plus side, we only heard from maybe 3 offended people.)

![Image](/assets/stories/3edc12c200.png)

We also goofed in making the first link out in the email go to the Web version of TL;DR. (tldr.inside.com), even though this was the destination to which we were least interested in sending people. 38.5% of all email clicks went to that first link, which didn’t do our download numbers for Chrome or iOS any good at all. Double oops.

So, it wasn’t a perfect launch, but we learned some valuable lessons, especially regarding email blasts. Namely, make sure the first link that appears in your email is the one you want people to use the most. Also, maybe think about a more clickable subject line that isn’t going to turn off or offend a large number of potential readers.

**When all was said and done, TLDR got about 5,000 additional new users based on our launch efforts Wednesday.** About 3,800 went to check out the Chrome Extension, which is what we were really pushing on Product Hunt. This was a big boost for us generally, and a great chance for the editorial team to show off all the great stories we’re covering and fun headlines we’re writing to a larger and more enthusiastic audience.

### We’re not done, of course. The app just came out and there’s tons of people we still want to show it to!

**Our next step:** Writing up a press release, contacting journalists, trying to get some blog posts written about us and do some interviews. Don’t worry: We’ll keep you posted on how all that goes as well.

— — — — — — — —

[**TLDR — iOS App**](https://itunes.apple.com/us/app/inside-tl-dr-24-7-breaking/id1042749369?mt=8)

# How $50 worth of Facebook Ads got my Startup a meeting with Pepsi’s execs

**Published:** 2014-07-28

![Image](/assets/stories/56c839d088.jpg)


## There’s always a creative way to use advertising


I’ve been watching a lot of [@garyvee](https://twitter.com/garyvee) videos lately, and he talks a lot about how you need to be marketing with a very specific goal. So I thought I’d share a story about how I took his words to heart, and made it happen for my startup.

**I’ll start this with a slight backstory as to why I was even trying to get ahold of Pepsi in the first place.**

A couple years ago, I was jogging with a [@stevenray](https://twitter.com/stevenray) at Katy Trail in Dallas. I’ll be straight with you guys “I’m out of shape”, so when I saw a vending machine in the distance my ears perked up. I took off running for this machine dreaming about freezing water and gatorade, but when I got there I realized that I didn’t have my wallet. I only had my phone, cause I left everything else in the car. I looked at [@stevenray](https://twitter.com/stevenray) and said “Why can’t I just log in to my Facebook, I’ll let them spam me forever, and they give me a free drink.” And that was the spark of the idea that became The LikeUs Network.

We ended up creating a startup that developed a piece of custom hardware that plugs into vending machines allowing them to accept mobile payment through ios, android, or a mobile browser. But more impressive, we allowed you to pay with **Social Currencies:** A Post on Facebook, A Tweet, Instagram Photo, X amount of Likes on a Instagram Photo, and pretty much any other piece of social marketing that any advertiser would want.

You can read more about [LikeUs Network here](https://www.stoodio.com/likeus-network-vending-mobile-payment-hardware/) or you can check out the [official LikeUs Network website](https://likeus.co/) here.

### So anyway…back to the Facebook Ads getting me a meeting at Pepsi.

So by this time we’re about 2 years into this startup. We’ve taken a ghetto idea, we got Mark Cuban to invest in our company, have a fully manufacturing ready piece of hardware that works in all of our test machines, and we’re ready to take on the vending world.


We should have been on top of the world, but there was always this weird thought in the back of my head. “Why isn’t Coke beating down my door, and why isn’t Pepsi tripping Coke to get in the front of the line with us?”

It kept me up at night wondering how we could have gotten a lot of press, been on tv, have small vendors calling us daily, but the big guys just didn’t seem to care.

## So I had an idea

Using Facebook’s social graph, I would setup an ad that would only show up to people who work at Pepsi & Coke. I would target an older demographic, cause I knew that their execs and decision makers weren’t gonna be straight out of college. And I would make the ad something that I know they don’t have.

The ad read like this:  
“No cash, No coin, No cards. Android, iOS, or any phone w/ a mobile browser.

![Image](/assets/stories/499e0448ba.png)

## I’m not joking when I say this…literally the next day I had a voicemail from a VP at Pepsi, wanted to talk about my product.

I quickly called him back, we had a great chat, he got excited, flew us out for a meeting with their tech/marketing/vending teams to see if this was something we could partner on.

1 day worth of a strategic Facebook advertisement instantly got rid of the void that was stuck in my head. There was no more wondering why they hadn’t called. I took a step, devized a plan, and I made them call me.

### What’s the point of this post?

The point isn’t to brag about how well we made out, cause we didn’t. After all the great things that happen with LikeUs Network, we still shut it down. The point is that you shouldn’t stay up at night wondering. You shouldn’t ask yourself why isn’t this company calling me for a huge merger. If that’s what you want, you need to come up with a plan, do some digging, and MAKE IT HAPPEN.

files.name: (2014) How I became a “Mexican Gypsy”.md
files.content:
# How I became a “Mexican Gypsy”

**Published:** 2014-10-22

![Image](/assets/stories/3b835c1dad.jpg)


## and how one phone call set it all in motion


I didn’t realize it when I was young, but I am a gypsy. I started calling myself a “Mexican Gypsy” a few years ago, and when I tell people how many cities I’ve lived in. They all agree. All of my friends and family, hell even my own wife, wonder what the hell is wrong with me. “Don’t you want to settle down, and put down roots?”. That’s pretty much the question I get everytime. I think to myself…of course I want to put down roots…own a house…and create a real home that my kids will grow up in. But quickly after that thought…I start thinking back to all the places I’ve lived…all the stories I have…all the friends we’ve made…and even all the businesses we’ve created over time. And it puts me into this purgatory state…where I want to push the limits of what “putting down roots” actually means.

> I’ve never been a fan of rules…I’ve never dreamed of working for someone else…and I hate when people tell me I can’t do something.

I’m currently on a plane heading to San Diego (the 14th city that me and my wife have lived in since we met) coming back from a trip to my hometown Dallas where I got to meet my new team at my new gig, see my dad, and chat with some close friends I never see anymore. As I’m sitting here watching a lightning storm right outside my window from a bird’s eye view…and the plane is shaking like crazy. For some reason that made me start thinking about the first time I moved. And I just now realized that the first time I moved away from texas was the move that create a whole new person and a whole new mindset in me. I thought I’d think back through that…and share some things that made me the **Mexican Gypsy**.

![Image](/assets/stories/5561670f68.jpg)

Back in 2002 I was a Visual Designer at Match.com. I was trying to split time playing baseball at the University of Texas at Dallas and working, but I quickly realized that school was actually getting in the way of the career I was going to school for. So I decided to go full-time career guy…and drop out of school. I’m not one of those people that regrets it…and I’m actually one of those people that think school is pretty pointless…and that everyone should just create companies…but that’s another post.

So I was working there about a year…and everything was great. I was moving up in the ranks…I was taking on tons of responsibility…and I loved going to work. I started getting really deep into analytics, affiliate marketing, and all of the online advertising approaches that the company & our affiliates were taking. I remember that our top affiliate was making over $1 million a year, and he moved to the philippines to work from paradise. I talked a lot with my friend and front-end developer Julio about how that was the dream I wanted. And it didn’t take long til that was just embedded in my head.

> “Why can’t I live in paradise…and make tons of money?”

At the same time I was dating this chick, who always told me how awesome Hawaii was. One day at work I randomly looked at Monster.com for things I thought would be cool for me, and there was a post for a “Director of Marketing for a New Dating Site”. Now I’m not a lead marketing person, nor was that my role at match AT ALL. But I was deep in the dating site game, and had this young cocky mindset that **I CAN DO ANYTHING**.

I applied…got a response…setup a phone interview…and honestly I can remember the entire call. More important than what was said…I remember that feeling of **“I’m killing this interview…and I’m selling my skills and knowledge like a fucking badass”**.

I told them about how they were going to hire a Director of Marketing but still need the rest of the team to be able to pull of creating a dating site from scratch. I sold them on hiring me to do what they originally wanted, design the whole site, hire a dev team, and take care of the product after launch. After some crazy background checks…and salary negotiations I got the gig. I remember being pumped and then I got a call the next day about how they wanted me to scan my degree from University of Texas Dallas. **Umm…remember a few paragraphs ago when I said that I left that shit behind.** Yeah well…I was gonna let that stop this amazing thing in my life from happening.

![Image](/assets/stories/ec9d6f803d.jpg)

I happened to have a friend that just graduated from UTD. I asked her if I could borrow her degree. She gave me a weird look, but agreed. And then I became a fraud. I was completely annoyed that even though they saw my work…they checked with my references over my skills and leadership…and they still wanted to see a degree. I know there are people out there that think you’re pretty much a piece of shit if you don’t have a degree, but I was determined to get this job…and prove to this person that degree or not. I am a badass, and that should be the deciding factor in hiring me.

So everything checked out…cause I got mad photoshop skills…and I had a few weeks before my big move. I started watching Blue Crush everyday (multiple times a day) so that I could “learn about hawaii”. I got everything ready…I already started looking for potential team members to hire…and most importantly buying cool sandals that I could wear everyday.


Then the day came. My dad took me to the airport…we cried a lot _(we’re criers…get over it)_…and then I was on my way to Hawaii. Some backstory there…I’m an only child, and my dad is a big part of my life. So it was a big deal for the only child to leave Texas for the first time and go 1500 miles away to an island. I’m sure my dad was scared…but I was determined.

![Image](/assets/stories/c8a33fd45d.jpg)

> _Kailua, HI_

I had a friend that lived in Hawaii…and the second I got off the plane we started looking at places to live. I had a week before I started my new gig…so had time to find my new home. The point of moving here was to experience paradise…so I wanted a place on or close to the beach. So much that I actually got a place that cost nearly $3k a month that was giant…just so I could live my dream. After a couple of days…I came to my senses…and talked the landlord into letting me out of my lease. She was cool with it and I remember her sitting me down and asking me if I wanted to get high with her.

So I ended up getting a little ghetto apartment…and starting this new gig. I met the owner, his wife, the wife’s brother (who was now my boss). Since the brother was now my boss I was curious what his background was. He told me that he’s been a restaurant manager for 20 years. I was like “oh do you guys do a lot of technical things in the background of the restaurants”. He’s like “No I just know how to manage people and get them to do their job!”. I pretty much knew this wasn’t the paradise fun trip I thought this was gonna be.

![Image](/assets/stories/76cbf22be3.jpg)

After a few weeks…I had gone through hundreds of resumes…had tons of interviews…and started to plan out the build of the new dating site. All while this is happening I found out from my old roommate that the girl I was dating was cheating on me. The job was horrible! My new boss’s approach for “managing me” was to sit directly behind me and just stare at me all day. If I opened up a chat…he’d ask why I wasn’t working. If I looked something up online…he’d ask why am I wasting time. If I got up to pee…he’d ask where I was going. I felt like I was in a prison or something. Finally I get my team hired and moved…and we are ready to start building this bad boy. At least that’s what I thought.

We came into work ready to knock this dating site out, and the owner came in to tell us he had changed his mind. He didn’t want to do a dating site anymore, he wanted to make a site where if you signup for a credit card and get approved we’ll give you a box of free hawaiian chocolate.

## Are you going WTF?  
That’s what we were thinking.

So my dream job in paradise was just falling into pieces. I got really upset…really homesick…and really scared that I just fucked up big time. I remember walking through Kapiolani Park talking to my dad…and telling him everything that was going on. Obviously we’re crying…that’s what we do. And my dad’s first thought was to just come home and it’s not a big deal. I was thinking the same thing too but I didn’t want to be some failure that gave up.

My next call was to my old creative director, [**Robert Boman**](https://twitter.com/robburrito). He could hear how upset I was…and understood all the bullshit…but he had a different approach to what he thought I should do. He straight up told me:

> _“You’re gonna deal with gigs like this through your life…and you’re gonna have shitty bosses. But it’s up to you to fight through it and get better…or to quit and stay the same. You will regret it if you come home now. I think you should stay there and stick it out.”_

I hung up the phone with a clear mind. I decided at that moment…that I was gonna stick it out…but more importantly that this wasn’t my last job ever…or the last product I was ever gonna have the chance to build. That this was an opportunity to work in a shitty place…but still work with a team I put together and “build something”.

The next day I just started to put this job as a secondary piece of my life to the paradise I was living in. I did my job…and I did it well…but right after work…I ran to the beach…played basketball with friends…and would take road trips around the island every single day. My unhappy scared self turned into a paradise-obsorbed happy person. I was with friends…in an amazing place…and I almost missed out on that.

That gig didn’t last long…cause the owner had crazy conversion expectations for his amazing chocolate site. And when it didn’t work out he let us go. But it had a silver lining. He gave us all a hefty severance package…so I got more free time to soak up Hawaii. Now I had this chunk of money to go anywhere I wanted to.

This beautiful island…this horrible boss…and a creative director with good advice…changed my life. One phone call changed my mindset…and I realized how any place I go from here on out is gonna have these amazing moments that stay with me forever.

I hate moving…but…I love absorbing a new place & creating the memories that come from those locations. And I owe so much to my old boss for being real with me. As I’m now a Creative Director and deal with teams…I think about this story all the time…when I want to tell people an easy way out. And it makes me realize that I’m not helping anyone grow if I don‘t tell them like it is.

> **_Jobs suck…Bosses suck…Sometimes Family & Friends suck. But if you put the shit in the background…and focus on the reason you’re there. You’ll always find something amazing that can change your life forever._**

**Where I’ve been since that first trip.  
**Dallas to Kailua Hawaii. Kailua Hawaii to Jersey City. Jersey City to Kihei Maui. Kihei to Honolulu. Honolulu to San Francisco. San Francisco to Vacaville. Vacaville to Oakland. Oakland to Waikiki. Waikiki to Austin. Austin to Round Rock. Round Rock to Dallas. Dallas to Walnut Creek. Walnut Creek to Irvine. Irvine to San Diego.

files.name: How Cuban Invested In Me.txt
files.content:
How Mark Cuban Invested In Me

It wasn't a pitch deck. It was a pacemaker and a basketball game.

I had a heart condition — needed a pacemaker at a young age. During recovery, I was watching a Mavericks game and tweeted something about it. Cuban replied. We started talking.

I told him about LikeUs, the social network I was building. He was interested. Not because of the pitch — because of the conversation. He could tell I was a builder, not a talker.

He invested. Not a huge check by Silicon Valley standards, but enormous for us. More importantly, he gave us credibility. "Mark Cuban-backed" opens doors that money alone can't.

The lesson: relationships matter more than pitch decks. Be genuine. Build in public. Let people see who you are before you ask them for money.

Also: tweet during basketball games. You never know who's watching.

files.name: The MySpace Hack.txt
files.content:
The MySpace Hack

2006. MySpace was everything.

I figured out you could embed Flash files in MySpace profiles. Not just decorative ones — functional ones. I built Flash widgets that looked like part of the profile but were actually affiliate links.

The trick was making them feel native. Users clicked because they thought they were interacting with the profile. They were actually clicking through to affiliate offers.

Made enough money to fund my next project. Learned a crucial lesson: distribution beats product every time. You can have the best product in the world, but if nobody sees it, it doesn't matter.

Also learned: platforms will always close loopholes. Build on your own ground when you can.

(MySpace eventually patched the Flash embed exploit. By then I'd moved on.)
